name: Kilkenny Castle
description: A Norman castle rebuilt in Victorian times, Kilkenny Castle dominates the medieval city from above the River Nore. Its picture gallery and formal gardens make it one of Ireland's most visited heritage sites.
history: Kilkenny Castle is a castle in Kilkenny, Ireland, built in 1260 to control a fording-point of the River Nore and the junction of several routeways. It was a symbol of Norman occupation, and in its original 13th-century condition, it would have formed an important element of the town's defences with four large circular corner towers and a massive ditch, part of which can still be seen today on the Parade.

name: Trim Castle
description: The largest Anglo-Norman castle in Ireland, Trim's massive keep was built by Hugh de Lacy. It served as a filming location for Braveheart and dominates the town from above the River Boyne.
history: Trim Castle is a castle on the south bank of the River Boyne in Trim, County Meath, Ireland, with an area of 30,000 m2. Over a period of 30 years, it was built by Hugh de Lacy and his son Walter as the caput of the Lordship of Meath. The Irish Government currently own and are in charge of the care of the castle, through the state agency The Office of Public Works (OPW).

name: Jerpoint Abbey
description: A Cistercian abbey founded in 1180 with some of the finest Romanesque and Gothic carvings in Ireland. Its sculptured cloister arcade features unique figures of knights, saints, and dragons.
history: Jerpoint Abbey is a ruined Cistercian abbey, founded in the second half of the 12th century in County Kilkenny, Ireland. It is located 2.5 km south west of Thomastown on the R448 regional road. There is a visitor centre with an exhibition. It has been declared a national monument and has been in the care of the Office of Public Works since 1880.

name: Lismore Castle
description: Perched on a cliff above the River Blackwater, Lismore was originally built by King John and later became a Devonshire seat. Its gardens are open to the public and the castle hosts a renowned art gallery.
history: Lismore Castle is a castle located in the town of Lismore, County Waterford, Ireland. It belonged to the Earls of Desmond, the Earls of Cork, and then to the Cavendish family from 1753. It is currently the Irish home of the Duke of Devonshire. The first castle on the site was built in 1185, and replaced, twice, in the 16th century. It was largely rebuilt in the Gothic style during the mid-nineteenth century for the 6th Duke of Devonshire.

name: Dunmore East Castle
description: A striking Martello-style fortification perched above the fishing village of Dunmore East, watching over Waterford Harbour with salt-sprayed battlements and sweeping Atlantic views.
history: Built in the early 19th century as part of coastal defences during the Napoleonic Wars, Dunmore East Castle (also called Duncannon Tower) guards the entrance to Waterford Harbour. The structure served as a signal station and defensive post, later falling into disuse as military threats receded.

name: Reginald's Tower
description: The oldest civic urban building in Ireland, this circular Norman tower stands at the apex of Waterford's Viking triangle. It has been in continuous use for over 800 years and now houses a museum.
history: Reginald's Tower is a historic tower in Waterford, Munster, Ireland. It is located at the eastern end of the city quay. The tower has been in usage for different purposes for many centuries and is an important landmark in Waterford and an important remnant of its medieval urban defence system. It is the oldest civic building in Ireland and it is the only urban monument in Ireland to retain a Norse or Viking name.

name: Dunbrody Abbey
description: A large Cistercian abbey founded by Hervé de Montmorency around 1170 on the banks of the River Barrow. Its impressive nave and crossing tower survive nearly to full height.
history: Dunbrody Abbey is a former Cistercian monastery in County Wexford, Ireland. The cross-shaped church was built in the 13th century, and the tower was added in the 15th century. With a length of 59m the church was one of the longest in Ireland. The visitor centre is run by the current Marquess of Donegall and has one of only two full sized hedge mazes in Ireland.

name: Ferns Castle
description: A brooding half-ruined Norman fortress in the quiet Wexford countryside, its surviving circular tower still imposing after eight centuries of storms and sieges.
history: Built around 1220 by William Earl Marshal on the site of an earlier Gaelic royal seat, Ferns Castle features a fine circular keep and rectangular towers. Ferns was once the capital of the Kingdom of Leinster under Diarmait Mac Murchada, whose invitation to the Normans changed Irish history forever. The castle was damaged in the 1641 Rebellion and abandoned by the 17th century.

name: Slade Castle
description: A tower house and fortified dwelling beside a small harbour on the Hook Peninsula. Its intact tower and bawn wall overlook the Celtic Sea at Ireland's southeastern tip.
history: Slade Castle is a tower house and National Monument in County Wexford, Ireland. It is located in the village of Slade on the Hook Peninsula and primarily dates to the late 15th or early 16th century.

name: Dunsany Castle
description: One of the oldest continuously inhabited houses in Ireland, home to the Plunkett family since the 12th century. The fantasy writer Lord Dunsany drew inspiration from its ancient stones and legends.
history: Dunsany Castle, Dunsany, County Meath, Ireland, is a modernised Anglo-Norman castle, started c. 1180 / 1181 by Hugh de Lacy, who also commissioned the original Killeen Castle, nearby, and the famous Trim Castle. It is one of Ireland's oldest homes in continuous occupation, possibly the longest occupied by a single family, having been held by the Cusack family and their descendants by marriage, the Plunketts, from foundation to the present day. The castle is surrounded by its demesne, the inner part of the formerly extensive Dunsany Estate. The demesne holds a historic church, a walled garden, a stone farm complex, and an ice house, among other features, and is home to a wide range of fauna.

name: Slane Castle
description: A Gothic Revival castle above the River Boyne, famous for its open-air rock concerts. Designed by James Gandon and Capability Brown, it houses an Irish whiskey distillery.
history: Slane Castle is located in the village of Slane, within the Boyne Valley of County Meath, Ireland. The castle has been the family seat of the Conyngham family since it was built in the late 18th century, on land first purchased in 1703 by Brig.-Gen. Henry Conyngham.

name: Dunmoe Castle
description: A crumbling Anglo-Norman tower overlooking the Boyne — where Ireland's ancient river valley meets five centuries of stone and silence.
history: A mid-15th century Anglo-Norman castle near Navan, County Meath, overlooking the River Boyne in the Boyne Valley. A National Monument of Ireland. The D'Arcy family held it for generations. Now a romantic ruin with impressive remaining tower walls.

name: Bective Abbey
description: A Cistercian abbey founded in 1147, once one of the most powerful in the Pale. Its fortified cloister was used as a film location for Braveheart.
history: Bective Abbey is a Cistercian abbey on the River Boyne in Bective, County Meath, Ireland.

name: Kells Priory
description: One of Ireland's largest and most impressive medieval monuments, enclosed by a massive fortified wall with towers. The Augustinian priory sprawls across several acres of riverside meadow.
history: Kells Priory is one of the largest medieval monuments in Ireland. The Augustine priory is situated alongside King's River beside the village of Kells in the townland of Rathduff (Madden), about 15 km south of the medieval city of Kilkenny. The priory is a National Monument and is in the guardianship of the Office of Public Works. One of its most notable features is a collection of medieval tower houses spaced at intervals along and within walls which enclose a site of just over 3 acres (12,000 m2). These give the priory the appearance more of a medieval fortress than of a place of worship and from them comes its local name of "Seven Castles".

name: Granny Castle
description: A haunting Butler stronghold overlooking the River Suir, its ivy-clad walls whispering tales of medieval power struggles in the golden vale of Kilkenny.
history: Built in the 14th century by the Butler family, Granny Castle (Grannagh) stands on a rocky outcrop above the River Suir near Waterford. The castle features a large rectangular tower house within a bawn wall. It served as a key defensive position controlling river traffic and was occupied until the 17th century Cromwellian period.

name: Clara Castle
description: One of Ireland's best-preserved tower houses, this imposing 15th-century fortress stands proud in the Kilkenny countryside with its original roof and battlements remarkably intact.
history: Built around 1450 by the Shortall family, Clara Castle is a fine example of a fortified tower house with its five storeys, murder holes, and original vaulted ceilings still intact. It passed through several families over the centuries. Recently restored, it offers a rare glimpse into authentic medieval Irish domestic life with original features preserved.

name: Dunmore Castle
description: Crumbling remains of a Butler fortress in the rolling Kilkenny countryside, where medieval walls emerge from tangled woodland like a forgotten dream.
history: Dunmore Castle in County Kilkenny was built by the Butler family in the medieval period. The town of Dunmore received its charter in the 14th century, and the castle served as a local stronghold. Though much reduced, the remaining walls and earthworks hint at its former importance as a centre of Butler power in the region.

name: Carlow Castle
description: Ireland's first towered keep — a revolutionary Norman design with massive corner towers flanking the River Barrow. Only two towers survive after a disastrous 19th-century demolition attempt, but their scale still takes your breath away.
history: Built 1207-1213, probably by William Marshal. The first castle of its kind in Ireland — a huge rectangular tower with four three-quarter-circular corner towers. National Monument. Much of the castle was accidentally destroyed in 1814 when a local doctor tried to reduce it to make room for a lunatic asylum.

name: Ballyloughan Castle
description: A towering Norman ruin near Mount Leinster with walls fifty feet high — a national monument whose early story has been swallowed entirely by time.
history: Built around 1300, this Norman castle near Bagenalstown, County Carlow is a National Monument. Features a twin-towered gatehouse, hall and corner tower foundations. The walls reach up to 50 feet high and 5 feet thick. Remarkably, nothing is recorded of its early history, though its features are typical of 13th-century construction.

name: Enniscorthy Castle
description: A magnificently restored Norman castle dominating the town of Enniscorthy, now housing a superb museum chronicling the turbulent history of County Wexford.
history: Originally built by Raymond le Gros in the 1190s, Enniscorthy Castle was granted to the Prendergast family before passing to the MacMurrough Kavanaghs. Rebuilt in the 16th century by Sir Henry Wallop, it played roles in the 1641 and 1798 rebellions. Extensively restored in 2011, it now houses the Enniscorthy Castle Museum exploring 1798, the Great Famine, and Easter 1916.

name: Ballyhack Castle
description: A powerful Knights Hospitaller tower house commanding the Waterford Estuary, its brooding bulk the origin of the Irish curse 'Go to Ballyhack.'
history: Built c.1450 by the Knights Hospitallers of St John, Ballyhack Castle overlooks the confluence of the rivers Barrow, Nore and Suir. The massive tower house was occupied by Cromwellian forces during the 1641 Rebellion and used as a deportation point under the 1652 Act of Settlement. Restored and now open to visitors with displays on the Crusades and Normans.

name: Rathmacknee Castle
description: A remarkably intact 15th-century tower house standing sentinel over the flat Wexford farmland, its five storeys and bawn wall beautifully preserved.
history: Built around 1450 by the Rossiter family, Rathmacknee is one of the best-preserved tower houses in County Wexford. The five-storey tower retains its battlements and sits within a large bawn with corner towers. It served as a key Rossiter stronghold until the Cromwellian period.

name: Burnchurch Castle
description: A tall, gaunt tower house rising from the Kilkenny pastures like a stone finger, its unusual height and intact battlements making it a striking medieval survivor.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Fitzgerald family, Burnchurch Castle is an impressively tall tower house standing six storeys high. The castle features fine battlements, a vaulted ground floor, and murder holes. It remained in the Fitzgerald family until the Cromwellian confiscations.

name: Athcarne Castle
description: A crumbling Elizabethan fortified house in the Meath countryside, its ornate carved doorway hinting at the ambitions of a Pale gentry family.
history: Built around 1590 by the Dowdall family, Athcarne Castle is a fortified house reflecting the transitional period between medieval defence and Renaissance comfort. The building features fine stone carvings and mullioned windows. It was damaged in the Confederate Wars and abandoned by the 18th century.

name: Ballymoon Castle
description: A vast, roofless Norman enclosure castle in the Carlow countryside—its enormous curtain walls enclosing nothing but sky, wildflowers, and eight centuries of silence.
history: Built around 1290, possibly by the Bigod or Carew families, Ballymoon Castle is one of the largest and most enigmatic Norman castles in Ireland. The massive rectangular enclosure with projecting towers has no keep—an unusual plan suggesting it was never completed. It was briefly held by the Kavanaghs and ruinous by the 16th century.

name: Grannagh Castle
description: A substantial Butler fortress on the banks of the River Suir, its impressive tower and curtain walls commanding the vital waterway between Kilkenny and Waterford.
history: Built by the Butler family in the 14th century, Grannagh Castle stands on the River Suir between Kilkenny and Waterford. The castle features a large tower house within a curtain wall and controlled river traffic on this important waterway. It served the Butlers as a strategic link in their chain of fortifications across the southeast.

name: Clomantagh Castle
description: A tall, slender tower house in the Kilkenny farmland, its dramatic profile visible for miles across the fertile Nore valley.
history: Clomantagh Castle is a 15th-century tower house in County Kilkenny, associated with the Shortall family. The tall, narrow tower retains much of its original height and features. It stands in rich agricultural land in the Nore valley and is characteristic of the many tower houses that dotted medieval Kilkenny.

name: Ballyragget Castle
description: A commanding tower house in the north Kilkenny market town, once a key outpost of Butler power on the frontier with Gaelic Laois.
history: Ballyragget Castle was built by the Butler family in the medieval period and served as an important stronghold on the northern frontier of their territory. The castle controlled the route from Kilkenny into the Gaelic territories of Laois and Offaly. The tower house retains significant structural elements and stands in the centre of the town.

name: Foulksrath Castle
description: A superbly restored Norman tower house near Kilkenny, now serving as a youth hostel where travellers sleep within 14th-century walls.
history: Built by the Purcell family in the 14th century, Foulksrath Castle near Jenkinstown was held by various Norman families. The well-preserved tower house features fine medieval details and has been restored and operated as An Oige (Irish Youth Hostel Association) accommodation, offering visitors the unique experience of staying in an authentic medieval castle.

name: Shankill Castle
description: An enchanting estate near Kilkenny where a medieval tower meets Georgian elegance, its walled gardens among the finest in the southeast.
history: Shankill Castle near Paulstown combines a 16th-century tower house with later Georgian additions, creating a harmonious blend of architectural periods. The estate has been home to the Cope family for over 300 years. The renowned walled gardens have been restored and opened to visitors, featuring formal plantings, a Victorian glasshouse, and woodland walks.

name: Johnstown Castle
description: A fairy-tale neo-Gothic castle rising from ornamental lakes and woodland in County Wexford — where Norman roots run deep beneath Victorian grandeur.
history: The first castle was a Norman tower house built in the late 12th century by the Esmonde family, who came from Lincolnshire after the 1169 Norman invasion. Confiscated during the Cromwellian era, acquired by John Grogan in 1692. The current neo-Gothic castle was created in the 19th century. Now houses the Irish Agricultural Museum and is surrounded by stunning ornamental gardens.

name: Bargy Castle
description: A fine fortified tower house in the Wexford Baronies, where the last speakers of the ancient Yola dialect once passed through its medieval doorway.
history: Bargy Castle is a medieval tower house in the Barony of Bargy, south Wexford. The Barony was notable for preserving the Yola language, an ancient form of Norman French-English, into the 19th century. The castle was held by various Anglo-Norman families and reflects the unique cultural heritage of this corner of Wexford that retained its Norman identity for centuries.

name: Clonmines
description: A ghostly deserted medieval town on the Wexford mudflats, its cluster of tower houses, churches, and monastery forming one of Ireland's most atmospheric lost settlements.
history: Clonmines was a prosperous Norman town in south Wexford that thrived in the medieval period as a port on the Bannow estuary. As the harbour silted up, the town was gradually abandoned. What remains is extraordinary: tower houses, two churches, an Augustinian priory, and town walls, all clustered together in an eerie landscape of mudflats and marsh.

name: Dunhill Castle
description: A brooding hilltop ruin overlooking the Waterford countryside — where the Powers held court and the Copper Coast stretches below in shades of green and grey.
history: A medieval castle ruin near Dunhill village in County Waterford, overlooking the Copper Coast. Built by the Power family who were prominent Anglo-Norman lords in the region. The ruins offer commanding views over the surrounding countryside.

name: Stradbally Castle
description: A medieval tower in the heart of Stradbally, Laois, known today more for the Electric Picnic festival that transforms its adjoining estate each September.
history: Stradbally Castle in County Laois was built by the O'More clan in the medieval period. The area later became part of the Cosby estate, whose grounds now host the Electric Picnic, one of Ireland's largest music festivals. The castle remains stand as a reminder of the area's turbulent medieval history when the O'Mores fiercely resisted English plantation.

name: Molana Abbey
description: A secluded island monastery on the Blackwater River, where ancient walls and a remarkable collection of medieval tombs lie hidden among the trees.
history: Founded in the 6th century on an island in the River Blackwater near Youghal, Molana Abbey became an Augustinian priory in the 12th century. The extensive ruins include a church, cloister, and fine collection of medieval tomb effigies. Raymond le Gros, the Norman knight who led the invasion of Ireland, is traditionally said to be buried here. The island setting adds to the atmosphere of seclusion.

name: Donore Castle
description: A compact medieval tower house in the Meath heartland near the Hill of Tara, standing watch over the storied landscape of ancient Irish kingship.
history: Donore Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath, located in the historically rich landscape near the Hill of Tara and the Boyne Valley. The tower dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences that characterised the English Pale's borderlands. The area's deep historical significance stretches back millennia.

name: Rathmiles Castle
description: A quiet tower house ruin in the Meath countryside, one of countless small medieval fortifications that once punctuated the rolling fields of the Pale.
history: Rathmiles Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath, dating to the 15th century. Like many similar structures in the Pale, it served as a fortified residence for a local landholding family. The tower retains partial walls and stands in agricultural land, a quiet reminder of the medieval landscape.

name: Duiske Abbey
description: A magnificently restored 13th-century Cistercian abbey in the charming Kilkenny town of Graiguenamanagh, still in active use as a parish church.
history: Founded in 1204 by William Marshal, 1st Earl of Pembroke, Duiske Abbey in Graiguenamanagh was one of the largest Cistercian abbeys in Ireland. After dissolution in the 16th century, the abbey fell into ruin but was gradually restored from the 1970s onward. It now serves as the parish church, with magnificent Romanesque and Gothic architectural details preserved.

name: Black Castle (Wicklow)
description: Dramatic Norman ruins perched on a rocky headland above Wicklow town harbour, battered by the Irish Sea for eight centuries.
history: Built by the FitzGerald family around 1176, Black Castle stands on a rocky promontory jutting into the sea at Wicklow town. The Norman fortress controlled the harbour and the surrounding coastline. The castle was attacked by Gaelic clans numerous times and was finally abandoned in the 17th century. The dramatic ruins, connected to the mainland by a narrow ridge, offer spectacular coastal views.

name: Kindlestown Castle
description: A crumbling Norman tower perched above the Wicklow coast — slowly being reclaimed by ivy and woodland while the Irish Sea crashes below.
history: A Norman castle ruin near Greystones in County Wicklow. Built by the de Ridelesfords, Norman lords granted lands in the area after the invasion. The remaining tower dates from the medieval period and stands in a wooded area overlooking the coast.

name: Killeen Castle
description: A magnificent Anglo-Norman castle in the Meath countryside, seat of the Plunkett Earls of Fingall for over 800 years, now a prestigious golf resort.
history: Killeen Castle near Dunsany, County Meath, was the seat of the Plunkett family, Earls of Fingall, for over 800 years. The castle was remodelled in the Gothic Revival style by James Shiel and Francis Johnston in the early 19th century. The estate now operates as a luxury golf resort, having hosted the 2011 Solheim Cup.

name: Summerhill Castle
description: Scant remains of a grand Palladian mansion in the Meath countryside, once considered the finest house in Ireland before its destruction.
history: Summerhill House in County Meath was built in the 1730s for Hercules Langford Rowley and was considered the finest Palladian mansion in Ireland. The house was burned in 1921 during the War of Independence and demolished in 1965. Only fragments of this once-magnificent building survive, making it one of Ireland's greatest architectural losses.

name: Balrath Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Meath countryside, part of the dense network of fortified residences that protected the English Pale.
history: Balrath Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath. Built in the 15th century, it was part of the network of tower houses that defended the English Pale around Dublin. The tower stands in the rich agricultural land of the Boyne Valley region.

name: Rathmore Castle
description: A tower house in the Meath hinterland, one of many medieval strongholds in the county that boasts Ireland's richest archaeological heritage.
history: Rathmore Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the defensive infrastructure of the Pale. Meath's extraordinary archaeological wealth, from Newgrange to medieval tower houses, makes it Ireland's premier heritage county.

name: Rataine Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Meath countryside near the Boyne Valley, Ireland's most archaeologically significant landscape.
history: Rataine Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath near the Boyne Valley. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the extraordinarily rich heritage landscape of the Boyne Valley, which includes Newgrange, Knowth, and Dowth passage tombs.

name: Ardmulchan Castle
description: Medieval ruins on the banks of the Boyne, where the famous river winds through a landscape layered with five millennia of human history.
history: Ardmulchan Castle stands on the banks of the River Boyne in County Meath. The medieval castle and nearby church ruins occupy a picturesque riverside position. The Boyne Valley is one of the most historically significant landscapes in Europe, with heritage stretching from Neolithic passage tombs to the 1690 Battle of the Boyne.

name: Liscartan Castle
description: A tower house near the Meath village of Liscartan, in the fertile heartland of the Pale where Anglo-Norman culture took deepest root.
history: Liscartan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the dense network of fortified residences in the Pale. The area was firmly within the English sphere of influence and the tower houses here reflect the settled agricultural prosperity of the region.

name: Knockmealdown Abbey
description: Monastic remains in the shadow of the Knockmealdown Mountains, on the Waterford-Tipperary border.
history: Knockmealdown Abbey is a monastic site in the foothills of the Knockmealdown Mountains on the Waterford-Tipperary border. The mountains themselves are named from the Irish 'Cnoc Maoldomhnaigh.' The abbey dates to the medieval period and sits in a scenic upland landscape.

name: Ballyquin Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the west Waterford countryside, near the Blackwater River and the scenic Dromana Gate.
history: Ballyquin Castle is a medieval tower house in County Waterford near the Blackwater Valley. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the Waterford countryside. The nearby Dromana Gate, a spectacular Hindu-Gothic folly, is one of Ireland's most unusual architectural features.

name: Knocktopher Abbey
description: Medieval monastic ruins in the pretty south Kilkenny village of Knocktopher, along the ancient pilgrim route through the Nore Valley.
history: Knocktopher Abbey in County Kilkenny was a Carmelite friary founded in the 13th century. The village sits on the ancient road through the Nore Valley. The abbey ruins include church remains with medieval architectural details. The village preserves its heritage character with an attractive streetscape.

name: Inistioge Castle
description: Castle remains in one of Ireland's most picturesque villages, where a graceful stone bridge spans the River Nore beneath wooded hillsides.
history: Inistioge in County Kilkenny is regularly voted one of Ireland's prettiest villages. The medieval castle and Augustinian priory ruins complement the village's beautiful setting on the River Nore. The village has been used as a filming location numerous times due to its photogenic character. A restored 18th-century bridge spans the river.

name: Grennan Castle
description: An imposing medieval fortress perched above the River Nore near Thomastown, commanding sweeping views across the Kilkenny countryside.
history: Grennan Castle near Thomastown in County Kilkenny is a substantial medieval castle overlooking the River Nore. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with the Butler family. Its elevated position above the river provides commanding views of the surrounding countryside and the heritage town of Thomastown.

name: Powerscourt Castle
description: Not the famous Wicklow estate, but the less-known Kilkenny medieval castle near the town of Piltown—a quieter footnote in the Butler saga.
history: Powerscourt Castle near Piltown in County Kilkenny is a medieval fortification associated with the Butler and Power families. The castle dates to the medieval period and should not be confused with the famous Powerscourt Estate in County Wicklow. It was part of the network of strongholds in south Kilkenny.

name: Russborough House
description: A magnificent Palladian mansion stretching 210 metres across the Wicklow landscape—the longest house frontage in Ireland, housing a world-class art collection.
history: Russborough House near Blessington was built 1741-1755 for Joseph Leeson, 1st Earl of Milltown, by architect Richard Cassels. Its 210-metre frontage makes it the longest house in Ireland. The house contains the Beit Art Collection, which was famously robbed multiple times including by Martin Cahill ('The General') in 1986. Now managed by the Alfred Beit Foundation.

name: Castletown Cox
description: An exquisite Palladian villa in the Kilkenny countryside, considered one of Ireland's most perfectly proportioned country houses.
history: Castletown Cox near Piltown, County Kilkenny, was designed by the great architect Davis Ducart for Michael Cox, Archbishop of Cashel, around 1767. The house is considered one of the finest Palladian villas in Ireland, with perfectly proportioned interiors. It remains in private hands and is not generally open to the public.

name: Ballysaggartmore Towers
description: Fantastical Gothic entrance lodges in the Waterford woodland, built to impress but bankrupting their owner before the mansion they heralded was ever built.
history: The Ballysaggartmore Towers near Lismore, County Waterford, are spectacular Gothic Revival gate lodges built around 1834 by Arthur Kiely-Usher. He spent so lavishly on these ornamental entrance lodges that he ran out of money before building the grand house they were meant to announce. The extravagant follies are now set in a public forest park.

name: Tacumshane Windmill
description: Ireland's only surviving complete thatched windmill, a charming 17th-century survivor standing in the flat Wexford farmland near the coast.
history: Tacumshane Windmill in County Wexford is Ireland's only complete surviving windmill, dating to 1846. The thatched windmill retains its sails and milling machinery. It stands in the flat agricultural landscape of south Wexford near Lady's Island, a traditional pilgrimage site.

name: Hook Lighthouse
description: The oldest operational lighthouse in the world, guiding mariners past the wild Wexford headland for over 800 years.
history: Hook Lighthouse on the Hook Peninsula, County Wexford, has been guiding ships for over 800 years, making it the oldest operational lighthouse in the world. The current tower was built by William Marshal around 1210 on a site where monks had tended a warning beacon since the 5th century. The phrase 'by hook or by crook' allegedly refers to the approaches to Waterford Harbour.

name: Loughcrew Cairns
description: A vast hilltop passage tomb cemetery in the Meath countryside, where Neolithic sun alignments illuminate Stone Age art each equinox.
history: The Loughcrew Cairns (Sliabh na Caillighe) in County Meath comprise over 30 passage tombs dating to around 3300 BC. The main cairn, Cairn T, contains remarkable megalithic art illuminated by the rising sun at the spring and autumn equinoxes. The complex rivals the more famous Boyne Valley sites and offers a more intimate, less crowded experience of Ireland's Neolithic heritage.

name: Hill of Slane
description: A sacred hilltop where St Patrick lit the Paschal Fire in 433 AD, challenging the High King of Tara and igniting the flame of Christianity across Ireland.
history: The Hill of Slane in County Meath is one of Ireland's most sacred sites. According to tradition, St Patrick lit the Paschal Fire here in 433 AD on Easter Eve, in direct defiance of the pagan festival at the Hill of Tara. This symbolic act is seen as the moment Christianity established itself in Ireland. The hill preserves a ruined friary, church, and college.

name: Kells Round Tower
description: A magnificent round tower and high crosses in the Meath town where the world-famous Book of Kells was kept for centuries.
history: The Round Tower at Kells, County Meath, is part of a monastic complex that housed the Book of Kells (now in Trinity College Dublin) for centuries after the monks fled Viking raids on Iona in the 9th century. The site also preserves several fine high crosses, including the remarkable South Cross with its biblical scenes. The tower and crosses together form one of Ireland's most important early Christian sites.

name: Clonard Castle
description: Remains near the ancient monastic site of Clonard in Meath, once one of Europe's greatest centres of learning in the Dark Ages.
history: Clonard in County Meath was one of the most important monastic sites in early Christian Ireland. Founded by St Finian in the 6th century, the monastery was reputedly attended by 3,000 students and produced the 'Twelve Apostles of Ireland.' The castle dates to the medieval period. Little remains of the original monastery but the site's significance in European intellectual history is immense.

name: Rathnageeragh Castle
description: A tower house in the Carlow countryside near the Barrow Valley, part of the medieval defence network of southeast Leinster.
history: Rathnageeragh Castle is a medieval tower house in County Carlow. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the defensive network in the Barrow Valley region of southeast Leinster. The area was contested between Anglo-Norman and Gaelic families throughout the medieval period.

name: Huntington Castle
description: A stunning 17th-century castle in County Carlow with medieval origins, famous for its Temple of Isis in the dungeon and magnificent gardens.
history: Huntington Castle near Clonegal, County Carlow, was built in 1625 by the Esmondes on the site of a Franciscan abbey. The castle has been continuously lived in by the same family for over 400 years. Its most unusual feature is a Temple of Isis established in the basement in 1976. The castle also features beautiful gardens and is said to be haunted.

name: Altamont Gardens
description: A hidden paradise of woodland gardens and formal plantings in the Carlow countryside, one of Ireland's most beautiful and romantic garden estates.
history: Altamont Gardens near Tullow, County Carlow, is one of Ireland's most beautiful gardens, covering 40 acres of formal gardens, woodland walks, and a lake. The gardens were largely created by Corona North, who tended them for decades. Now managed by the OPW, the gardens feature ancient oaks, rhododendrons, rare plants, and a atmospheric walk to the River Slaney.

name: Ardmore Cathedral
description: An extraordinary assemblage of early Christian treasures on a wild Waterford headland—round tower, cathedral, ogham stones, and cliff-edge holy well.
history: Ardmore, County Waterford, is one of Ireland's oldest Christian sites, associated with St Declan who may have predated St Patrick. The site includes a magnificent round tower, a 12th-century cathedral with remarkable Romanesque arcading depicting biblical scenes, ogham stones, and St Declan's holy well on the cliff edge. The round tower is one of the best preserved in Ireland.

name: Mahon Falls Tower
description: A signal tower near the dramatic Mahon Falls in the Comeragh Mountains, in one of Waterford's most spectacular mountain landscapes.
history: The tower near Mahon Falls in the Comeragh Mountains, County Waterford, is a Napoleonic-era signal tower, part of the coastal defence system built in the early 19th century. The nearby Mahon Falls cascade dramatically down a corrie headwall. The area is popular for hillwalking in the wild Comeragh landscape.

name: Strangely Castle
description: A tower house ruin near Dunmore East in County Waterford, overlooking the dramatic Waterford coastline.
history: Strangely Castle is a medieval tower house near Dunmore East, County Waterford. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the coastal landscape of south Waterford. The tower is in a ruinous state but retains some structural features.

name: Curraghmore House
description: The seat of the Marquis of Waterford—Ireland's largest private demesne, where a magnificent Georgian mansion sits amid 2,500 acres of parkland and ancient woodland.
history: Curraghmore House near Portlaw, County Waterford, is the ancestral seat of the de la Poer family (Marquesses of Waterford) and claims to be the oldest continuously inhabited house in Ireland. The estate covers 2,500 acres. The shell house in the grounds is one of Ireland's finest garden follies. The house opens to visitors on selected dates.

name: Cappoquin Castle
description: A medieval castle overlooking the River Blackwater in one of Waterford's prettiest towns, where the river makes a dramatic turn southward toward the sea.
history: Cappoquin Castle stands above the River Blackwater in the town of Cappoquin, County Waterford. The town sits at the point where the Blackwater makes its famous 90-degree turn southward. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with the FitzGerald and Keating families.

name: Woodstown Castle
description: A tower house near the shores of Waterford Harbour, close to where a major Viking settlement was discovered in the 2000s.
history: Woodstown Castle is a medieval tower house near Waterford Harbour. Nearby, one of Ireland's most significant Viking archaeological discoveries was made in the 2000s when road construction revealed an extensive 9th-century Viking camp. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the historically rich landscape around Waterford Harbour.

name: Callan Friary
description: Augustinian friary ruins in the medieval Kilkenny town of Callan, birthplace of the great Irish rebel priest Edmund Ignatius Rice.
history: Callan Friary in County Kilkenny was an Augustinian foundation. The town of Callan is notable as the birthplace of Edmund Ignatius Rice (1762-1844), founder of the Christian Brothers and the Presentation Brothers. The friary ruins and medieval town heritage reflect Callan's importance in medieval Kilkenny.

name: Thomastown Castle
description: Castle remains in the charming Kilkenny town of Thomastown, a medieval riverside settlement that has become a centre for contemporary craft.
history: Thomastown, named after the Norman Thomas FitzAnthony, preserves medieval castle and town wall remains. The town sits on the River Nore and has developed a reputation as a centre for contemporary craft, particularly through the Grennan Mill Craft School and nearby Jerpoint Park. The medieval bridge and streetscape add to its heritage character.

name: Gowran Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the south Kilkenny town of Gowran, where a superb medieval church preserves remarkable carved stone effigies.
history: Gowran in County Kilkenny preserves medieval castle remains and an exceptional medieval church (Collegiate Church of St Mary) with outstanding carved stone tomb effigies of the 14th century. The church is considered one of the finest medieval parish churches in Ireland. Gowran is also known for its racecourse.

name: Tullaherin Round Tower
description: A round tower stump and medieval church ruin in the Kilkenny countryside, near the remarkable medieval village of Kells.
history: Tullaherin Round Tower in County Kilkenny is the stump of an early Christian round tower beside medieval church ruins. The site is near Kells Priory, one of Ireland's largest and most impressive medieval monastic enclosures. The round tower dates to the 9th-10th century and marks an early Christian monastic foundation.

name: Kilfane Church
description: A medieval church in the Kilkenny countryside containing one of Ireland's finest carved medieval effigies—a Norman knight in full armour known as 'The Long Cantwell.'
history: Kilfane Church in County Kilkenny preserves one of Ireland's most remarkable medieval stone effigies: the 'Long Cantwell,' a nearly 2.5-metre carved figure of a Norman knight in chain mail armour dating to the 13th century. The effigy is among the finest surviving examples of medieval funerary sculpture in Ireland.

name: Graiguenamanagh Abbey
description: See Duiske Abbey—this historic Kilkenny town on the River Barrow is one of Ireland's most attractive small towns.
history: Graiguenamanagh on the River Barrow is home to Duiske Abbey, founded in 1204. The town straddles the Kilkenny-Carlow border and is one of Ireland's most attractive riverside settlements. The restored abbey, medieval bridge, and mountain backdrop of Brandon Hill create an exceptionally scenic heritage town.

name: Granagh Castle
description: A substantial medieval castle on the banks of the River Suir in County Waterford, commanding a strategic river crossing.
history: Granagh Castle on the River Suir in County Waterford is a substantial medieval castle that controlled a river crossing. The castle dates to the medieval period and features significant structural remains including walls and towers. Its riverside position made it strategically important.

name: Castle Kevin
description: A medieval tower in the Wicklow countryside, in the shadow of the mountains that sheltered early Christian monks at nearby Glendalough.
history: Castle Kevin is a medieval tower house in County Wicklow, in the foothills of the Wicklow Mountains. The area has deep historical connections to the great monastic site of Glendalough. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the defence of the Pale's southern frontier against the O'Toole and O'Byrne clans of Wicklow.

name: Athlumney Castle
description: A dramatic castle ruin on the hill above the River Boyne near Navan, deliberately burned by its owner to prevent it falling to William of Orange.
history: Athlumney Castle near Navan, County Meath, was burned by its owner Sir Lancelot Dowdall after the Battle of the Boyne in 1690, to prevent it being used by the victorious Williamite forces. The dramatic act of defiance left a magnificent shell combining a 15th-century tower house with a later Elizabethan mansion. The ruins overlook the Boyne Valley.

name: Skryne Church
description: Medieval church ruins on the Hill of Skreen in County Meath, with legendary connections to the early spread of Christianity in Ireland.
history: The Hill of Skreen (Skryne) in County Meath was an important early Christian site. The medieval church ruins stand on a hill with views toward the Hill of Tara. According to tradition, St Patrick's disciple Colmcille founded a church here. The hill has deep archaeological significance in the Tara landscape.

name: Hill of Tara
description: The ancient seat of the High Kings of Ireland—a sacred hilltop where kingship, mythology, and archaeology converge in the most symbolically charged landscape in the country.
history: The Hill of Tara in County Meath was the ceremonial and political capital of ancient Ireland, seat of the High Kings for millennia. The site preserves the Lia Fail (Stone of Destiny), the Mound of the Hostages (a Neolithic passage tomb c.3000 BC), and extensive earthworks. Daniel O'Connell held a monster meeting here in 1843. Its symbolic significance in Irish identity is unmatched.

name: Black Abbey Kilkenny
description: A beautifully restored Dominican priory in the heart of medieval Kilkenny, still in active religious use with a stunning rosette window.
history: The Black Abbey in Kilkenny was founded in 1225 and is one of the oldest Dominican foundations in Ireland. The priory takes its name from the black cloaks of the Dominican friars. After dissolution and centuries of use as a courthouse, it was restored to religious use in 1866. The magnificent rosette window and medieval architecture make it one of Kilkenny's treasures.

name: St Canice's Cathedral
description: Kilkenny's magnificent medieval cathedral with Ireland's second-tallest round tower, offering panoramic views of the medieval city from its ancient summit.
history: St Canice's Cathedral in Kilkenny dates to the 13th century and is the second-longest medieval cathedral in Ireland. The adjacent round tower, dating to the 6th-9th century, is the second tallest in Ireland and can be climbed for spectacular views. The cathedral contains remarkable medieval tomb effigies and architectural details spanning 800 years of history.

name: Dungarvan Castle
description: A compact but impressive Norman castle guarding the harbour of Dungarvan, one of Waterford's most vibrant and foodie-friendly towns.
history: Dungarvan Castle was built by King John around 1185 and stands guard over the harbour of this attractive Waterford town. The castle features a large circular keep within a polygonal curtain wall. Dungarvan has become one of Ireland's premier food destinations, and the castle adds medieval character to the bustling harbour town. Managed by the OPW.

name: Tybroughney Castle
description: A riverside tower house on the Tipperary-Kilkenny border, overlooking the confluence of the Suir and its tributaries.
history: Tybroughney Castle is a medieval tower house on the banks of the River Suir near Piltown, on the Tipperary-Kilkenny border. The castle dates to the medieval period and controlled a section of the Suir. Its riverside setting reflects the strategic importance of waterway control in medieval Ireland.

name: Duncannon Fort
description: A massive star-shaped fortress commanding the approaches to Waterford Harbour, where four centuries of military engineering have created an extraordinary coastal stronghold.
history: Duncannon Fort on the Hook Peninsula, County Wexford, is a large star-shaped fort built in 1588 to counter the Spanish Armada threat. It was expanded over the following centuries and saw action in the Confederate Wars and the 1798 Rebellion. The fort remained in military use until 1922. Its commanding position at the mouth of Waterford Harbour and massive stone defences make it one of Ireland's most impressive military heritage sites.

name: Leighlinbridge Castle
description: A Norman castle on the banks of the River Barrow, where one of Ireland's oldest bridges crosses a river that has been a highway since prehistoric times.
history: Leighlinbridge Castle in County Carlow stands beside one of Ireland's oldest bridges over the River Barrow. Built around 1180 by the Normans, the castle controlled this important river crossing. The attractive village preserves its medieval character with the castle, bridge, and riverside setting creating a harmonious heritage scene.

name: Clonegal Castle
description: A castle in the Carlow-Wexford border village of Clonegal, close to the ancient Wicklow Gap pass into the mountains.
history: Clonegal Castle in County Carlow stands in a picturesque village on the River Derry at the border of Carlow and Wexford. The castle dates to the medieval period and was near the route through the Wicklow Mountains. The village retains a quiet charm with its riverside setting.

name: Wicklow Gaol
description: A chilling 18th-century prison now a museum, where the horrors of transportation, the 1798 Rebellion, and the Great Famine come vividly to life.
history: Wicklow Gaol, built in 1702, now operates as an award-winning heritage museum. The gaol held prisoners including 1798 rebels and transported convicts. The museum uses actors, sound effects, and multimedia to recreate the harrowing conditions. The Great Famine section is particularly moving. It is one of Ireland's most popular heritage attractions.

name: Glendalough Monastic Site
description: A mystical early Christian monastery nestled in the 'Valley of Two Lakes' — round tower piercing the Wicklow sky, Celtic crosses standing sentinel among ancient graves.
history: Founded in the 6th century by St Kevin, who sought solitude in this remote glacial valley. Grew into one of Ireland's great centres of learning, producing manuscripts in Irish and Latin. Surviving buildings date from the 10th-12th centuries, including the iconic 30m round tower. Repeatedly raided by Vikings and finally destroyed by English forces in 1398. The pilgrimage site never fully died.

name: Baltinglass Abbey
description: Cistercian abbey ruins in the Wicklow town of Baltinglass, where medieval monks built on a hilltop already sacred for thousands of years.
history: Baltinglass Abbey in County Wicklow was a Cistercian foundation established in 1148 by Dermot MacMurrough, King of Leinster. The abbey ruins include fine Romanesque architectural details. The nearby hilltop preserves a Neolithic passage tomb, creating a landscape where Christian and pre-Christian sacred sites coexist.

name: Rathdrum Castle
description: Castle remains in the Wicklow village of Rathdrum, boyhood home of Charles Stewart Parnell, the 'uncrowned king of Ireland.'
history: Rathdrum in County Wicklow has castle remains and is best known as the location of Avondale House, childhood home of Charles Stewart Parnell (1846-1891), the great nationalist leader known as the 'uncrowned king of Ireland.' Avondale House and Forest Park are now a major visitor attraction.

name: Taghmon Castle
description: A medieval tower and church ruin in the Westmeath village of Taghmon, preserving early Christian heritage in the Irish midlands.
history: Taghmon in County Westmeath preserves medieval castle and church remains. The name derives from 'Teach Munna' (House of St Munna), indicating early Christian monastic origins. The village retains its heritage character in the quiet Westmeath midlands.

name: Kilrossanty Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Waterford countryside, in the scenic foothills of the Comeragh Mountains.
history: Kilrossanty Church is a medieval ruin in County Waterford near the Comeragh Mountains. The church dates to the medieval period and served the local parish. The Comeragh Mountains, with their dramatic corrie lakes and mountain trails, provide a scenic backdrop.

name: Oldcastle Meath
description: A heritage town near the Loughcrew Cairns in northwest Meath, gateway to one of Ireland's most important Neolithic landscapes.
history: Oldcastle in County Meath is the gateway to the Loughcrew Cairns, an extraordinary Neolithic passage tomb complex dating to c.3300 BC. The town serves as a base for exploring this ancient landscape. The area also contains numerous medieval and later heritage sites.

name: Navan Castle
description: Norman castle remains in the county town of Meath, in the heart of Ireland's richest archaeological landscape.
history: Navan, the county town of Meath, has Norman castle remains. The town sits at the confluence of the Rivers Boyne and Blackwater, a strategically important position since prehistoric times. The area surrounding Navan contains extraordinary archaeological sites including the Hill of Tara and the Boyne Valley tombs.

name: Rathmoylan Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Waterford countryside near the coast, in an area of quiet rural beauty.
history: Rathmoylan Church is a medieval church ruin in County Waterford. The church dates to the medieval period and stands in the coastal hinterland of south Waterford, an area of quiet agricultural landscapes and scattered heritage sites.

name: Woodstock Castle Kilkenny
description: The ruins of a Victorian mansion set in fine gardens and woodland parkland near Inistioge, with panoramic views across the Nore Valley.
history: Woodstock House near Inistioge, County Kilkenny, was a grand Victorian mansion set in 50 acres of gardens and woodland. The house was burned in 1922 during the Civil War. The gardens and arboretum have been restored and offer stunning views across the Nore Valley. The shell of the house and the restored gardens form an atmospheric heritage park.

name: Shee Alms House
description: A rare surviving medieval almshouse in the heart of Kilkenny city, one of the finest examples of medieval charitable architecture in Ireland.
history: The Shee Alms House on Rose Inn Street, Kilkenny, was founded in 1582 by Sir Richard Shee to provide accommodation for the poor. It is one of very few surviving Tudor almshouses in Ireland. The building has been beautifully restored and now serves as a tourist information office while preserving its remarkable medieval architecture.

name: Rothe House
description: A magnificent Tudor merchant's house in the heart of medieval Kilkenny—the finest surviving example of its kind in Ireland, now a museum.
history: Rothe House on Parliament Street, Kilkenny, was built between 1594 and 1610 by the Rothe family, wealthy merchants of Kilkenny. The complex of three houses connected by courtyards is the only surviving example of a Tudor merchant's townhouse in Ireland. Now a museum and genealogical centre, it preserves Kilkenny's rich medieval and Tudor heritage.

name: Coolhill Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Kilkenny countryside near Inistioge, in one of Ireland's most scenic riverside landscapes.
history: Coolhill Castle is a medieval tower house in County Kilkenny near Inistioge. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the beautiful Nore Valley landscape, one of the most scenic areas of southeast Ireland.

name: Inistioge Church
description: Medieval church remains in one of Ireland's prettiest riverside villages, complementing the heritage streetscape of this charming Kilkenny settlement.
history: Inistioge Church is part of the medieval heritage of Inistioge village, County Kilkenny. The village's medieval churches, castle, and priory, combined with its beautiful bridge and riverside setting on the Nore, make it one of Ireland's most photogenic and frequently filmed villages.

name: Castlemorris House
description: A Georgian house in the Kilkenny countryside, reflecting the elegance of 18th-century landed gentry life in southeast Ireland.
history: Castlemorris House in County Kilkenny is a Georgian country house reflecting the refined lifestyle of the 18th-century Irish landed gentry. The house stands in the productive agricultural landscape of Kilkenny.

name: Ballybur Castle
description: A substantial medieval tower house near Kilkenny city, one of the many fortified residences that ring the medieval city of the Marble City.
history: Ballybur Castle near Kilkenny city is a substantial medieval tower house. The castle dates to the 15th century and was one of numerous tower houses surrounding Kilkenny, the medieval capital of the Butler lordship.

name: Kilmeaden Castle
description: A tower house near the Waterford city suburbs, in the valley where the famous Kilmeaden cheese was once produced.
history: Kilmeaden Castle is a medieval tower house near Kilmeaden village, County Waterford. The village lends its name to the popular Kilmeaden cheese brand. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Annestown Castle
description: A tower house near the scenic Waterford coastline, overlooking one of the Copper Coast's beautiful cove beaches.
history: Annestown Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Annestown on the Waterford coast. The area is part of the Copper Coast UNESCO Global Geopark, named for 19th-century copper mining. The dramatic coastline features sea stacks, coves, and geological formations.

name: Duleek Priory
description: Medieval priory ruins in one of Ireland's oldest Christian settlements, where St Patrick is said to have built the first stone church in Ireland.
history: Duleek in County Meath is one of Ireland's earliest Christian settlements. St Patrick reportedly established the first stone church in Ireland here. The priory ruins date to the 12th century and include fine architectural details. Duleek is also notable for its medieval wayside cross.

name: Newgrange Cursus
description: Part of the Brú na Bóinne UNESCO World Heritage Site — where 5,000-year-old passage tombs predate the pyramids and the winter solstice still illuminates the dead.
history: Part of the Brú na Bóinne complex in the Boyne Valley, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Newgrange was built c.3100 BC — older than Stonehenge and the Egyptian pyramids. A passage tomb with a roof box that channels sunlight at winter solstice. The surrounding monuments include henges, passage graves, and ringforts spanning millennia.

name: Knowth Passage Tomb
description: A 5,000-year-old Neolithic mound holding Europe's largest collection of megalithic art — older than the pyramids, with two mysterious passages aligned to the equinox sunrise and sunset.
history: Built c. 3200 BC during the Neolithic era, part of the Brú na Bóinne UNESCO World Heritage complex alongside Newgrange and Dowth. Contains two passage tombs — eastern and western — within the Great Mound, surrounded by 17 smaller satellite tombs. Houses over one-third of all Western European megalithic art on its 127 decorated kerbstones. Used continuously through Bronze Age, Iron Age, and Early Christian periods.

name: Dowth Hall
description: A Georgian mansion near the famous Dowth passage tomb in the Boyne Valley, in the heart of one of Europe's most important archaeological landscapes.
history: Dowth Hall in County Meath stands near Dowth passage tomb, one of the three great Neolithic tombs of the Boyne Valley (alongside Newgrange and Knowth). The Georgian hall is part of a later landscape layer in this extraordinarily multi-period heritage zone.

name: Dromana Gate
description: One of the most extraordinary follies in Ireland—a spectacular Hindu-Gothic gate lodge spanning the entrance to the Dromana estate above the Blackwater.
history: The Dromana Gate near Cappoquin, County Waterford, is one of Ireland's most remarkable architectural follies—a stunning Hindu-Gothic gateway built in 1849, inspired by the Brighton Pavilion. The exotic gate leads to the Dromana estate overlooking the River Blackwater. It was built as a temporary decoration for the return of the estate owners but proved so popular it was made permanent.

name: Kilbarry Castle Waterford
description: A tower house near Waterford city, now surrounded by the expanding suburbs of Ireland's oldest city.
history: Kilbarry Castle is a medieval tower house near Waterford city. The castle dates to the medieval period and once stood in open countryside that has been absorbed by Waterford's suburban expansion.

name: Coolhull Castle Wexford
description: A well-preserved tower house in the Wexford countryside, retaining its original features and defensive character.
history: Coolhull Castle in County Wexford is a well-preserved medieval tower house retaining many original features.

name: Mayglass Tower
description: A tower house in the south Wexford countryside, in the Barony of Forth where the ancient Yola language survived until the 19th century.
history: Mayglass Tower is a medieval tower house in south County Wexford, in the historic Barony of Forth where the ancient Yola language (a form of Norman French-English) survived into the 19th century.

name: Kilcloggan Castle
description: A tower house in the Wexford countryside near the coast, part of the dense Norman settlement of southeast Ireland.
history: Kilcloggan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Wexford.

name: Lady's Island Castle
description: Castle ruins on a tidal island in County Wexford, a major medieval pilgrimage site still visited by devotees each August.
history: Lady's Island in County Wexford has castle and church ruins on a tidal island in a lake. The island has been a major Marian pilgrimage site since the medieval period, with annual pilgrimages still taking place each August. The castle was an Anglo-Norman fortification.

name: Selskar Abbey
description: A substantial Augustinian abbey in the heart of Wexford town, where Henry II did penance for the murder of Thomas Becket.
history: Selskar Abbey in Wexford town was an Augustinian foundation where Henry II of England spent Lent 1172 doing penance for the murder of Thomas Becket. The substantial ruins stand in the town centre. The abbey was an important religious house throughout the medieval period.

name: Ferrycarrig Castle
description: A Norman tower on a rocky promontory at the mouth of the River Slaney, near the Irish National Heritage Park.
history: Ferrycarrig Castle near Wexford town stands on a rocky promontory at the mouth of the River Slaney. The Norman tower dates to the 12th century. Nearby is the Irish National Heritage Park, which recreates 9,000 years of Irish settlement from the Mesolithic to the Norman period.

name: Dowdstown Castle Meath
description: A tower house in the rich Meath countryside, part of the medieval infrastructure of the English Pale.
history: Dowdstown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath, part of the Pale's defensive network.

name: Rathmullan Castle Meath
description: A tower house in County Meath, in the historically rich landscape of the Boyne Valley.
history: Rathmullan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath near the Boyne Valley.

name: Drumconrath Castle
description: Castle remains in the north Meath village of Drumconrath, near the border with County Louth.
history: Drumconrath Castle is a medieval fortification in County Meath.

name: Gormanston Castle
description: A grand castle and estate on the Meath coast, now a Franciscan college, seat of the Viscounts Gormanston who hold Ireland's oldest surviving peerage.
history: Gormanston Castle in County Meath is the seat of the Viscounts Gormanston, holders of Ireland's oldest surviving peerage (created 1478). Legend says foxes gather and bark at the castle when a Viscount dies. The estate now houses a Franciscan school. The castle combines medieval and later elements.

name: Painestown Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Meath countryside, in the rich archaeological hinterland of the Boyne Valley.
history: Painestown Church is a medieval church ruin in County Meath. The church stands in the archaeologically rich landscape of the Boyne Valley.

name: Piercetown Castle
description: A tower house in the Wexford countryside, part of the dense Norman settlement pattern of southeast Leinster.
history: Piercetown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Wexford.

name: Ballylaneen Castle
description: A tower house in the Waterford countryside between Dungarvan and Tramore.
history: Ballylaneen Castle is a medieval tower house in County Waterford.

name: Clonea Castle Waterford
description: A tower house near the popular Clonea Strand beach in County Waterford.
history: Clonea Castle is a medieval tower house near Clonea Strand, one of Waterford's finest beaches.

name: Knockmealdown Castle
description: A fortification in the shadow of the Knockmealdown Mountains on the Waterford-Tipperary border.
history: Knockmealdown Castle is near the Knockmealdown Mountains, a scenic range on the Waterford-Tipperary border.

name: Freshford Church
description: A medieval church in the Kilkenny village of Freshford, with a fine Romanesque doorway.
history: Freshford Church in County Kilkenny features a notable Romanesque doorway dating to the 12th century.

name: Clogh Castle Kilkenny
description: A tower house in north Kilkenny, near the border with County Laois.
history: Clogh Castle is a medieval tower house in County Kilkenny.

name: Tullaroan Castle
description: A tower house near the Kilkenny village of Tullaroan, famous as the heartland of hurling in Ireland.
history: Tullaroan Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Tullaroan, County Kilkenny. Tullaroan is famous in the GAA world as one of the great heartlands of hurling, having produced numerous All-Ireland champions.

name: McKenna's Castle
description: A fortification in the Ulster borderlands associated with the McKenna clan.
history: McKenna's Castle is a fortification associated with the McKenna clan in the Ulster border region.

name: Mothel Abbey
description: Medieval abbey ruins in the Waterford countryside.
history: Mothel Abbey is a medieval monastic site in County Waterford.

name: Kilkieran High Crosses
description: A remarkable collection of early Christian high crosses near Kilkieran in County Tipperary, featuring unusual west-country Romanesque carvings.
history: The Kilkieran High Crosses in County Tipperary are a group of early Christian crosses near the village of Kilkieran. The crosses feature distinctive carvings and represent an important school of early medieval stone carving in Munster.

name: Castlecomer Castle
description: Castle remains in the Kilkenny coal-mining town of Castlecomer, where the Wandesforde family developed Ireland's only significant coalfield.
history: Castlecomer Castle in County Kilkenny was the seat of the Wandesforde family who developed the Castlecomer coalfield, Ireland's only significant coal deposits. The mining heritage is now celebrated in the Castlecomer Discovery Park.

name: Fertagh Round Tower
description: A fine round tower near Johnstown in County Kilkenny, one of the best-preserved examples in the southeast.
history: The Fertagh Round Tower near Johnstown, County Kilkenny, is a well-preserved early Christian round tower marking a monastic site. The tower retains good height and structural integrity.

name: Old Leighlin Cathedral
description: A fine medieval cathedral in the quiet Carlow village of Old Leighlin, preserving Romanesque and Gothic architectural details.
history: Old Leighlin Cathedral in County Carlow is a medieval cathedral with origins in the 7th century. The current building dates mainly to the 12th-13th century and features Romanesque and Gothic details. The cathedral serves a tiny parish but contains rich architectural heritage.

name: Clonmore Castle Carlow
description: A medieval castle and round tower in the Carlow village of Clonmore, where the ancient Sli Cualann route crossed the border into Wicklow.
history: Clonmore in County Carlow preserves castle remains and a round tower on an ancient route into the Wicklow Mountains. The monastic site was founded by St Mogue in the 6th century.

name: Dunlavin Castle
description: Castle remains in the west Wicklow town of Dunlavin, a pretty market town with a fine planned square.
history: Dunlavin in County Wicklow has castle remains and a distinctive planned market square with a courthouse. The town sits in the western Wicklow foothills.

name: Rathgall Fort
description: One of the largest hillforts in Ireland, a massive multi-vallate enclosure in the Carlow-Wicklow borderlands with Bronze Age origins.
history: Rathgall Fort near Tullow, County Wicklow, is one of the largest hillforts in Ireland. The massive multi-vallate enclosure dates to the Late Bronze Age (c.1000 BC). Excavations revealed evidence of bronze-working, suggesting it was a major metalworking centre. The site covers over 7 hectares.

name: Newcastle McKynegan
description: A medieval castle in the Wicklow coastal town of Newcastle, guarding the route between Dublin and Wicklow along the Irish Sea coast.
history: Newcastle (McKynegan) in County Wicklow has medieval castle remains. The town sits on the coast road between Dublin and Wicklow, historically an important and often dangerous route through the territory of the O'Byrne and O'Toole clans.
